query: does atemoya juice affect CYP3A4 enzyme activity

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Cytochrome P450 -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Due to the presence of CYP enzymes in intestinal wall tissue, diseases of the small and large bowels can affect CYP enzymes as well. While the brush-border enzymes and other parts of the intestines play a limited role in the metabolism of drugs and other xenobiotics, their role in drug absorption is their most significant contributing feature. This activity can play a factor in altering the oral bioavailability of drugs, rendering some treatments ineffective or greater than expected, and creating greater responsibility on the clinician's part to dose the medication to provide therapeutic benefit appropriately. Grapefruit juice is an example of a natural compound that affects drug metabolism at the site of the intestinal wall in small amounts. Absorbed by the small intestine, a natural flavonoid found in grapefruits known as naringin acts locally to inhibit CYP3A4 enterocytes, potentially lead to greater plasma concentrations of 3A4-mediated drugs. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Cytochrome P450 -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: All individuals on drug therapy should receive an evaluation to check for any concurrent medications that may influence the activity of another drug based on their effects on CYP activity. Based on the characteristics of the drug's profile, CYP enzymes can be either induced or inhibited-marking a significant area of concern in patients due to issues with metabolism and clearance. This can cause considerable adverse effects for patients as medications may either accumulate to toxic levels or clear from the system too rapidly, leading to treatment failure. Also, clinicians must be aware of naturally occurring compounds that can alter the actions of CYP enzymes, such as grapefruit juice, nicotine-containing products, and St. John’s Wort, to name a few. Special precautions are necessary for patients who have decreased baseline activity or injury to CYP enzymes.

retrieved_docs.title: Drug Distribution -- Clinical Significance -- Drug: Grapefruit juice
retrieved_docs.content: CYP enzyme: CYP3A4 inhibitor Drug-Drug Interaction: buspirone Metabolizing Enzyme: CYP3A4 Side Effects: Dizziness and serotonin syndrome caused by increased buspirone level.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Cytochrome P450 -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: With the advent and increasing popularity of herbal medicines and supplements, many people are now subjecting themselves to natural compounds that have not undergone rigorous scientific investigation to understand their side effect profile fully. Part of the worry behind people taking these supplements is that they have unknown effects both on the human body and in combination with prescription medications. For example, St. John’s Wort, an herbal remedy anecdotally believed to help treat depression and pseudodementia, is a known inducer of CYP3A4 and can lead to decreased plasma concentration of various prescription medicines. On the other hand, grapefruit juice is a known inhibitor of CYP3A4 and can have opposite effects. Nicotine is another compound that induces CYP1A2, and both nicotine and caffeine serve as substrates to CYP1A2, explaining a potential mechanism behind the phenomenon of increased tolerance to caffeine among smokers. [5] [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Midazolam -- Adverse Effects -- Drug-Drug Interactions
retrieved_docs.content: CYP3A4 inhibitors: Taking midazolam with moderate or strong CYP3A4 inhibitors can lead to prolonged sedation because these drugs reduce its clearance. Avoid using midazolam with moderate or strong inhibitors, and be cautious when using it with mild inhibitors. Examples of moderate inhibitors include verapamil, clarithromycin, and erythromycin. Strong inhibitors include itraconazole, diltiazem, and ketoconazole. Monitoring is also necessary for drug interactions with protease inhibitors, rifampin, phenytoin, and phenobarbital. Induction and inhibition of CYP450 3A4 play a role in decreased and increased drug levels in the circulation. Grapefruit juice reduces the activity of the CYP 450 enzyme and increases the level of the drug. St. John's wort induces the enzyme and reduces the blood level of midazolam. [1] [35] [16]

retrieved_docs.title: Drug Distribution -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: The cytochrome P-450 system is a family of heme-containing enzymes found in the liver and intestinal tract. There are multiple forms of CYP enzymes. Some drugs can either induce or inhibit specific isoforms of the enzyme, affecting the ADME of a drug. A clinician must be aware of potential drug-drug interactions with CYP enzyme inducers and inhibitors and naturally occurring compounds that can alter the actions of CYP enzyme. Naturally occurring compounds include grapefruit juice, nicotine-containing products, and St. John’s wort. [13] [14] Below is a list of the major drugs that inhibit and induce the cytochrome P-450 system, as well as dugs that are a major substrate of the enzyme:

retrieved_docs.title: Calcium Channel Blockers -- Adverse Effects -- Drug-Drug Interactions
retrieved_docs.content: Many drug-drug interactions involving CCBs result from their extensive first-pass metabolism via the CYP450 enzyme system. The following summarizes some of the potential interactions with CCBs. [14] Reduced doses of interacting medications may be needed. CYP3A isoenzyme inhibition by verapamil and diltiazem: Ciclosporin Statins Benzodiazepines Buspirone Sildenafil Inhibition of CYP3A isoenzyme: Cimetidine Erythromycin Clarithromycin Azole antifungals Protease inhibitors Grapefruit juice Induction of CYP3A isoenzyme: Carbamazepine Oxcarbazepine Phenytoin Nevirapine Rifampicin Pioglitazone P-glycoprotein inhibition by verapamil and diltiazem Carbamazepine Ciclosporin Fexofenadine Daunrubicin

retrieved_docs.title: Oxybutynin -- Adverse Effects -- Drug-Drug Interactions
retrieved_docs.content: CYP3A4 primarily metabolizes oxybutynin. Caution should be exercised when using potent CYP3A4 inhibitors, such as ketoconazole, as they may increase the plasma concentration of oxybutynin. [28] Likewise, other inhibitors of the CYP3A4 enzyme, such as itraconazole, miconazole, clarithromycin, erythromycin, and grapefruit juice, have the potential to affect the plasma concentration of oxybutynin; therefore, concurrent use of these substances requires caution. [29] [30] [31] Oxybutynin can decrease gut motility, which may reduce the effectiveness of prokinetic drugs, such as metoclopramide. Caution should be exercised while using oxybutynin concurrently with other anticholinergic drugs, as they can lead to an increased risk of adverse effects, such as xerostomia (dry mouth), constipation, and urinary retention. [32]

retrieved_docs.content: CYP450 enzyme induction by drugs like phenobarbital, carbamazepine Smoking High protein and low carbohydrates diet Barbecued meat Age range 4 to 16 Factors that decrease clearance are: Old age P450 enzyme inhibition Liver disease Infectious diseases like pneumonia or viral infection Congestive heart failure

retrieved_docs.title: Estrogen(Archived) -- Adverse Effects -- Drug-Drug Interactions
retrieved_docs.content: CYP3A4 inhibitors: The inhibitors of CYP3A4 can increase plasma concentrations of estrogens, resulting in a higher incidence of estrogen-related adverse effects. Notable CYP3A4 inhibitors include erythromycin, clarithromycin, ketoconazole, itraconazole, ritonavir, and grapefruit juice. CYP3A4 inducers: In vitro and in vivo studies have demonstrated that CYP3A4 partially metabolizes estrogens. Inducers of CYP3A4 may decrease plasma concentrations of estrogens, leading to reduced therapeutic efficacy and alterations in the uterine bleeding profile. Common inducers of CYP3A4 include St. John’s Wort, phenobarbital, carbamazepine, and rifampin. Tizanidine : Ethinyl estradiol in combined oral contraceptives inhibits CYP1A2, leading to reduced presystemic metabolism of tizanidine and significantly increased plasma levels and pharmacodynamic effects. Coadministration may enhance tizanidine-induced hypotension; caution is warranted.

retrieved_docs.title: Amylase -- Interfering Factors
retrieved_docs.content: Amylase assays typically resist hemoglobin, bilirubin, or triglyceride interference. However, collecting specimens in tubes containing oxalate, citrate, or EDTA may result in falsely decreased values due to the chelation of necessary amylase cofactors. [3] Certain medications, such as aspirin, morphine, antiretrovirals, and estrogen-containing drugs, can potentially affect serum amylase levels. [28]

retrieved_docs.title: Drug Elimination -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: Phase I involves oxidation, reduction, and hydrolysis of the exogenous molecule. These reactions are accomplished by hepatic microsomal enzymes, which reside in the smooth endoplasmic reticulum of the hepatocytes. Best known among them is the cytochrome P450 system, whose enzymes are predominantly involved in oxidative metabolism.  Within the cytochrome P450 family (CYP), the enzyme responsible for the metabolism of more than 50% of existing drugs is the CYP3A4. Its activity encompasses various classes of medications, including opioids, immunosuppressants, antihistamines, and benzodiazepines. The enzymes can also be induced or inhibited by a variety of substances they interact with, including pharmaceuticals. The increase in metabolic activity with CYP induction results in a diminished activity of drugs targeted by that particular isoform. Conversely, CYP inhibition will result in increased drug plasma concentration, potentially leading to toxicity. The CYP3A4 is induced by phenytoin, phenobarbital, and St. John's wort, while diltiazem, erythromycin, and grapefruit inhibit it.  Caution is, therefore, necessary when administering CYP3A4-metabolized drugs in the presence of any of the inhibitors or inducers. [3] [4]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 4 -- Community 2776
retrieved_docs.content: The human cytochrome P450 3A4 protein is an important metabolic enzyme that catalyzes the biotransformation of the proton‑pump inhibitor omeprazole. By oxidizing omeprazole, CYP3A4 influences the drug’s plasma concentration, efficacy, and clearance, making it a key factor in determining appropriate dosing and in predicting potential drug‑drug interactions. Variability in CYP3A4 activity—whether due to genetic differences, concomitant medications, or hepatic function—can alter omeprazole metabolism, affecting therapeutic outcomes and the risk of adverse effects. Understanding that CYP3A4 metabolizes omeprazole is essential for clinicians when optimizing treatment regimens, especially in patients receiving multiple CYP3A4 substrates or inhibitors.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 1667
retrieved_docs.content: Amiodaron acts as an inhibitor of the cytochrome P450 enzymes CYP2C9 and CYP3A. By suppressing the activity of these metabolic pathways, amiodaron can alter the clearance of drugs that are substrates of CYP2C9 or CYP3A, increasing the risk of drug‑drug interactions. Recognizing amiodaron’s inhibitory effects on both CYP2C9 and CYP3A is essential for clinicians when selecting and dosing concomitant medications, to ensure safe and effective patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 4 -- Community 2705
retrieved_docs.content: Amiodarone and omeprazole are both classified as inhibitory enzymů, meaning each drug functions as an enzyme inhibitor. This shared pharmacologic property highlights their potential to affect metabolic enzyme activity, which is important for anticipating drug‑drug interactions and guiding appropriate dosing in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 3 -- Community 2102
retrieved_docs.content: Amiodarone (spelled “amiodaron” in the source data) is a substrate of the human cytochrome P450 3A4 enzyme, which means CYP3A4 metabolizes amiodarone as it does other drugs such as omeprazole. In addition, amiodarone functions as an inhibitor of cytochrome P450 enzymes CYP2C9 and the CYP3A family (including CYP3A4). Consequently, amiodarone can both be cleared by CYP3A4 and simultaneously reduce the metabolic activity of CYP2C9 and CYP3A enzymes, potentially altering the plasma concentrations of co‑administered medications that depend on these pathways. This dual role as a substrate and inhibitor is important for clinicians to consider when prescribing amiodarone together with other drugs metabolized by CYP2C9 or CYP3A4, such as omeprazole, to avoid adverse drug interactions and ensure optimal patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 2389
retrieved_docs.content: Amiodaron is both a substrate and an inhibitor of the cytochrome P450 enzyme system. Specifically, amiodaron is metabolized by the human cytochrome P450 3A4 protein, an enzyme that metabolizes a wide range of chemicals and drugs. At the same time, amiodaron is classified as an inhibitory cytochrome P450 CYP3A agent, meaning it can suppress the activity of CYP3A enzymes. This dual role as a substrate of CYP3A4 and an inhibitor of CYP3A highlights the potential for amiodaron to affect the metabolism of other concurrently administered medications, making awareness of drug‑drug interactions essential in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 3 -- Community 2668
retrieved_docs.content: Amiodarone is metabolized by the human cytochrome P450 3A4 enzyme, and it also acts as an inhibitor of cytochrome P450–mediated metabolism. Omeprazole is likewise a substrate of cytochrome P450 3A4, and it possesses inhibitory activity toward certain cytochrome P450 enzymes. Because both amiodarone and omeprazole share the CYP3A4 metabolic pathway and can inhibit CYP enzymes, they have the potential to interact with other drugs that are metabolized by this enzyme system. Recognizing these overlapping metabolic and inhibitory properties is essential for anticipating drug‑drug interactions and optimizing patient safety.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 3 -- Community 1369
retrieved_docs.content: The human cytochrome P450 3A4 protein metabolizes the chemotherapeutic agent paclitaxel and the proton‑pump inhibitor omeprazol, indicating that CYP3A4 activity is a key determinant of the clearance of these drugs. Amiodaron is also a substrate of the same CYP3A4 enzyme, so its plasma levels are influenced by factors that induce or inhibit CYP3A4. In addition, amiodaron acts as an inhibitor of the cytochrome P450 isoform CYP2C9, which can diminish the metabolic capacity of CYP2C9 for other co‑administered medications. Consequently, drugs that are metabolized by CYP2C9 may achieve higher concentrations when given with amiodaron, while agents that affect CYP3A4 can alter amiodaron exposure. Recognizing these interconnected metabolic pathways is essential for anticipating drug‑drug interactions and optimizing patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 1797
retrieved_docs.content: Amiodaron and mikonazol are members of the class of inhibitory cytochrome P450 enzymes CYP2C9 and CYP3A, meaning both drugs act as inhibitors of these key metabolic pathways. By inhibiting CYP2C9 and CYP3A, amiodaron and mikonazol can markedly alter the clearance of other medications that are substrates of these enzymes. One clinically important consequence of amiodaron’s enzyme inhibition is its contraindication with the antiretroviral agent saquinavir; co‑administration is avoided because amiodaron’s inhibition of CYP3A (and potentially CYP2C9) can lead to elevated saquinavir concentrations and increased risk of toxicity. Recognizing that both amiodaron and mikonazol share this inhibitory profile helps clinicians anticipate drug‑drug interactions and guide safe prescribing practices.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 4 -- Community 2835
retrieved_docs.content: Amiodaron is identified as an inhibitor of the cytochrome P450 enzyme CYP2C9, which can alter the metabolic clearance of drugs that are substrates of this pathway. Because of this inhibitory effect, amiodaron is contraindicated for use with saquinavir, a protease inhibitor that relies on CYP2C9 for its metabolism. The interaction between amiodaron’s CYP2C9 inhibition and saquinavir’s metabolic requirements can lead to elevated saquinavir levels and increased risk of toxicity. Clinicians should therefore avoid co‑prescribing amiodaron and saquinavir and consider alternative therapies or dose adjustments when managing patients who require either medication.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 4 -- Community 2635
retrieved_docs.content: Amiodarone is contraindicated in patients receiving nelfinavir mesylate because of a significant drug interaction. In addition, amiodarone functions as an inhibitor of the cytochrome P450 enzymes CYP2C9 and CYP3A, which can alter the metabolism of other medications that are substrates of these enzymes. The combination of its contraindication with nelfinavir mesylate and its inhibitory effects on CYP2C9 and CYP3A highlights the importance of thorough medication review and monitoring when amiodarone is prescribed.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 3 -- Community 2012
retrieved_docs.content: Amiodarone is an antiarrhythmic drug that functions as an inhibitor of the hepatic enzyme cytochrome P450 CYP2C9. By suppressing CYP2C9 activity, amiodarone can alter the metabolic clearance of other medications that are substrates of this enzyme, leading to increased plasma concentrations and a heightened risk of adverse effects. Awareness of amiodarone’s inhibitory effect on CYP2C9 is essential for clinicians when prescribing concomitant drugs metabolized by this pathway, in order to manage potential drug‑drug interactions and ensure safe patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 117
retrieved_docs.content: pharmacologic class relationships and CYP3A4‑mediated contraindications are essential for safe prescribing and for preventing serious adverse events such as hypotension, excessive drug exposure, and statin‑related muscle toxicity.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 2278
retrieved_docs.content: Mikonazol and amiodaron are agents that act as inhibitors of the metabolic enzymes described as inhibitory cytochromu P450 CYP2C9 and inhibitory cytochromu P450 CYP3A. Both inhibitory cytochromu P450 CYP2C9 and inhibitory cytochromu P450 CYP3A play key roles in the biotransformation of a wide variety of drugs. By inhibiting these enzymes, mikonazol and amiodaron can decrease the metabolic clearance of other medications that are substrates of CYP2C9 or CYP3A, potentially leading to increased plasma concentrations and heightened risk of adverse effects. Recognizing the inhibitory effects of mikonazol and amiodaron on inhibitory cytochromu P450 CYP2C9 and inhibitory cytochromu P450 CYP3A is important for clinicians to anticipate and manage possible drug‑drug interactions in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1531
retrieved_docs.content: The human cytochrome P450 3A4 protein metabolizes mikonazol, while amiodaron is also a substrate of the cytochrome P450 3A4 protein, indicating that CYP3A4 is responsible for the clearance of both agents. In addition, both mikonazol and amiodaron act as inhibitors of the enzymes described as inhibitory cytochromu P450 CYP2C9 and inhibitory cytochromu P450 CYP3A, meaning they can suppress the activity of these CYP isoforms. Consequently, the metabolism of other drugs that depend on CYP2C9 or CYP3A may be reduced when patients are treated with mikonazol or amiodaron, highlighting the importance of monitoring for potential drug‑drug interactions in clinical care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 3 -- Community 1323
retrieved_docs.content: Omeprazole and cyclosporine are both classified as enzyme inhibitors. By inhibiting key metabolic enzymes, particularly members of the cytochrome P450 system, these agents can alter the biotransformation of concurrently administered medications, increasing the risk of drug‑drug interactions and affecting therapeutic efficacy. Recognizing the enzyme‑inhibitory properties of omeprazole and cyclosporine is essential for clinicians when selecting and dosing concomitant drugs, monitoring for adverse effects, and optimizing patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 2 -- Community 911
retrieved_docs.content: Human cytochrome P450 3A4 protein metabolizes both mikonazol and amiodaron, indicating that these drugs are substrates of this major hepatic enzyme. In addition, both mikonazol and amiodaron act as inhibitors of inhibitory cytochromu P450 CYP2C9 and inhibitory cytochromu P450 CYP3A, meaning they can decrease the metabolic activity of these isoforms and potentially alter the clearance of other co‑administered medications that depend on these pathways. Furthermore, the mechanism of action of amiodaron includes adrenergic beta‑antagonist activity, as described by the Adrenergic beta‑Antagonists [MoA] relationship. Together, these pharmacokinetic and pharmacodynamic interactions highlight the importance of careful drug‑interaction monitoring and dose adjustment when mikonazol, amiodaron, or other CYP3A4, CYP2C9, or CYP3A substrates are used concurrently in patient care.
